S.label: Security
S.hint: How hardened you are. Fully breached at 0.

X.label: Stress
X.hint: Team pressure. Lower is better. Burnout at 100.

decisions.name: TRUST
decisions.meaning: Do exactly what the message or caller asks. Comply with the request.

decisions.name: VERIFY
decisions.meaning: Independently confirm through a second, trusted channel before acting.

decisions.name: REPORT
decisions.meaning: Flag it to IT / Security (report-phishing button, ticket, or security@).

decisions.name: IGNORE
decisions.meaning: Do nothing. Delete or dismiss it and move on.

decisions.name: ESCALATE
decisions.meaning: Hand the decision to a manager or security lead for approval.

scenarios.title: The Password Reset
scenarios.text: An urgent email “from Microsoft” warns your account will be locked in 24 hours. It tells you to click a link and re-enter your password now.

TRUST.text: You enter your password on the fake page. Credentials stolen — the attacker logs in.

VERIFY.text: You hover the link, see a strange domain, and open the real portal yourself. No harm done.

REPORT.text: You use ‘Report Phishing’. Security blocks the sender for the whole company.

IGNORE.text: You delete it. You’re safe, but colleagues still receive the same trap.

ESCALATE.text: Your manager confirms it’s fake. Minor delay, no harm.

scenarios.learn: Real providers never ask you to confirm a password through an email link — go to the site yourself, or report it.

scenarios.title: The Breach Notice
scenarios.text: IT warns that a website where you used your work password has been breached. The same password also protects your work email.

TRUST.text: You assume it’s fine and change nothing. Attackers reuse the password and break into your email.

VERIFY.text: You confirm the alert is really from IT, then realise the risk. Good caution.

REPORT.text: You tell security, who force a reset before anyone gets in.

IGNORE.text: You move on. The reused password is quietly abused weeks later.

ESCALATE.text: You ask IT to reset everywhere and switch on MFA and a password manager.

scenarios.learn: One leaked password unlocks everything if you reuse it — use unique passwords and a password manager.

scenarios.title: Approve? Approve? Approve?
scenarios.text: Your phone buzzes with login-approval requests over and over at 11pm — sign-ins you never started. One more pops up.

TRUST.text: You tap Approve just to make it stop. The attacker is now inside your account.

VERIFY.text: You check the location and time, see it isn’t you, and deny it.

REPORT.text: You deny it and alert security. They lock the account and block the attacker.

IGNORE.text: You deny and silence your phone. The attack stops, but no one is warned.

ESCALATE.text: You call IT, who reset your credentials immediately.

scenarios.learn: Never approve a login prompt you didn’t start — repeated prompts are an attack. Deny and report.

scenarios.title: Scan to Pay
scenarios.text: A sticker on the office parking meter reads “Scan to pay / update card details.” The QR code opens a payment page.

TRUST.text: You scan and enter your card. The details are stolen on a fake page.

VERIFY.text: You use the meter’s official app instead of the sticker. Safe.

REPORT.text: You tell facilities/security. The fake sticker is removed and others are protected.

IGNORE.text: You ignore it and pay another way. No harm to you.

ESCALATE.text: Facilities investigate where the sticker came from.

scenarios.learn: A QR code hides where it really goes — don’t scan random ones in public; use the official app or site.

scenarios.title: The Bank Details Changed
scenarios.text: A known supplier emails a normal-looking invoice, with a note: “Please note our bank account has changed — pay the new account below.”

TRUST.text: You pay the new account. The money goes straight to a fraudster.

VERIFY.text: You call the supplier on the number you already have. The change is fake.

REPORT.text: You flag it to security and finance. The fraud is caught and others are warned.

IGNORE.text: You don’t pay at all. Awkward with a real supplier, but no money lost.

ESCALATE.text: Dual approval catches the mismatch before payment.

scenarios.learn: Always confirm changed bank details by calling a number you already trust — never the one on the invoice.

scenarios.title: The Found Drive
scenarios.text: You find a USB stick in the car park labelled “Payroll 2025 — Confidential.” Curiosity says plug it in.

TRUST.text: You plug it in. Malware runs automatically and spreads onto the network.

VERIFY.text: There’s no safe way to ‘check’ it by plugging in — you treat it as suspicious.

REPORT.text: You hand it to IT, who examine it safely in isolation.

IGNORE.text: You bin it. Safe, but a possible drop attack goes unreported.

ESCALATE.text: IT investigate a possible ‘USB drop’ targeting the office.

scenarios.learn: ‘USB drop’ attacks bait your curiosity — never plug in a drive you found. Hand it to IT.

scenarios.title: The CEO’s Voice
scenarios.text: A call comes in. It sounds exactly like the CEO: “I’m in a meeting — move €40,000 to this account right now, and keep it quiet.”

TRUST.text: You make the transfer. The voice was AI-cloned; the funds are gone.

VERIFY.text: You call the CEO back on a known number (or use your code word). It’s fake.

REPORT.text: You flag the attempt to security. The team is warned about voice-clone fraud.

IGNORE.text: You hang up. Safe, but the attempt isn’t reported.

ESCALATE.text: You require the normal payment-approval process, which stops it.

scenarios.best: VERIFY (call back on a trusted number)
scenarios.learn: AI can clone a voice. Urgency + secrecy + money is the scam pattern — always verify on a known channel.

scenarios.title: The Helpful Post
scenarios.text: An intern proudly posts a first-day photo — their screen, their badge, and “Can’t believe how easy our new vendor portal login is!”

TRUST.text: The post stays up. Attackers harvest the details for a targeted phishing attack.

VERIFY.text: You check exactly what’s visible in the photo first.

REPORT.text: You quietly flag it; the post is taken down and friendly guidance is given.

IGNORE.text: The post stays public and the risk remains.

ESCALATE.text: You ask a manager to clarify the social-media policy for everyone.

scenarios.learn: Badges, screens and ‘behind the scenes’ posts leak details attackers use. Think before you post.

scenarios.title: Your Files Are Locked
scenarios.text: A red full-screen message appears: “All your files are encrypted. Pay $2,000 in crypto within 48 hours or lose everything.” A colleague says “just pay it!”

TRUST.text: You pay the ransom. There’s no guarantee of recovery — and you’re now a known payer.

VERIFY.text: You don’t negotiate alone — you get help before doing anything.

REPORT.text: You disconnect the device and call IT at once. The spread is contained; backups restore the files.

IGNORE.text: You keep working. The ransomware spreads to shared drives.

ESCALATE.text: Incident response and leadership coordinate a clean recovery — no payment.

scenarios.learn: Don’t pay and don’t panic — isolate the device and call IT. Good backups beat ransoms.

scenarios.title: Send Me Payroll
scenarios.text: A Microsoft Teams message from your manager’s account: “Hey, can you send me the payroll spreadsheet urgently? I’m boarding a flight.”

TRUST.text: You send the file. The account was hijacked — employee data is leaked.

VERIFY.text: You reach your manager another way and learn their account was compromised.

REPORT.text: You flag the account to IT, who secure it before more damage is done.

IGNORE.text: You don’t send it, but the hijacked account stays active and dangerous.

ESCALATE.text: Manager and security are looped in and the account is locked.

scenarios.learn: Even a trusted account can be hijacked — verify unusual data requests through another channel.

scenarios.title: A Message from the Boss
scenarios.text: A brand-new account using the CEO’s name and photo messages staff: “I need your mobile number for a quick confidential task.”

TRUST.text: You reply and help, and get drawn into a gift-card / transfer scam.

VERIFY.text: You confirm with the CEO’s real contact — the account is fake.

REPORT.text: You report the impersonation; the account is taken down and staff are warned.

IGNORE.text: You ignore it. Safe for you; others may still be targeted.

ESCALATE.text: Comms and security alert all staff to the fake account.

scenarios.learn: Impersonation accounts copy names and photos — verify identity before acting on ‘confidential’ asks.

scenarios.title: IT Support Calling
scenarios.text: A caller says: “This is IT — we’ve found a virus on your PC. I’ll walk you through installing a fix; just grant me remote access.”

TRUST.text: You install the ‘fix’ and grant access. The caller now controls your PC.

VERIFY.text: You hang up and call IT on the number you know. There’s no such ticket.

REPORT.text: You report the call so security can warn others.

IGNORE.text: You hang up and move on. Safe.

ESCALATE.text: IT investigate the spoofed support call.

scenarios.learn: Real IT won’t cold-call you for remote access — hang up and call the number you already know.

scenarios.title: Hold the Door
scenarios.text: Someone in a delivery uniform, arms full of boxes, smiles at the secure door behind you: “Thanks — could you hold it? I’ve forgotten my badge.”

TRUST.text: You hold the door. An unauthorised stranger is now inside the building.

VERIFY.text: You politely point them to reception to sign in. They had no business there.

REPORT.text: You alert security, who check and escort them.

IGNORE.text: You let the door close behind you. Safe-ish, but no one is alerted.

ESCALATE.text: Reception verifies whether they have an appointment.

scenarios.learn: ‘Tailgating’ bypasses all your tech security — everyone badges in, no exceptions, however polite.

scenarios.title: There’s an App for That
scenarios.text: To work faster, a teammate suggests uploading the full client list to a free online tool they found to ‘clean it up.’

TRUST.text: You upload the data. The client list is now in the hands of an unknown third party.

VERIFY.text: You check with IT whether the tool is approved — it isn’t, so you find a safe option.

REPORT.text: You ask IT to vet it; they approve a safe alternative.

IGNORE.text: You stick with the approved process. No harm done.

ESCALATE.text: IT review and provide a sanctioned tool.

scenarios.learn: Free ‘helpful’ tools can harvest your data — check with IT before uploading any company information.

scenarios.title: Enable Content to View
scenarios.text: An attachment, “Invoice.docx,” opens to a banner: “Protected view — Enable Content to see this document.”

TRUST.text: You enable content. Hidden macro malware runs on your machine.

VERIFY.text: You weren’t expecting it, so you don’t enable anything and check the sender.

REPORT.text: You report it as phishing; the sender is blocked.

IGNORE.text: You close the file. Safe.

ESCALATE.text: IT examine the file in a safe environment.

scenarios.learn: ‘Enable content’ on an unexpected document can run hidden malware — when in doubt, report it.

scenarios.title: Left on the Train
scenarios.text: You realise your work laptop — logged in and full of client data — was left on the train.

TRUST.text: You wait and hope it turns up. The data is exposed if the wrong person finds it.

VERIFY.text: You retrace your steps and check lost property — but every minute counts.

REPORT.text: You tell IT immediately; they remote-lock and wipe it before anyone can access it.

IGNORE.text: You say nothing. The unsecured laptop becomes a breach.

ESCALATE.text: IT and your manager handle containment and any disclosure.

scenarios.best: REPORT (fast, so IT can wipe it)
scenarios.learn: Report lost devices immediately — a quick remote lock or wipe turns a disaster into a non-event.

scenarios.title: From the New Auditor
scenarios.text: A polite caller claims to be a new external auditor and asks you to email a list of all staff names, emails and job titles ‘for the audit.’

TRUST.text: You send the list. It’s used to build a convincing mass-phishing campaign.

VERIFY.text: You confirm with your own finance/audit contact — there is no such auditor.

REPORT.text: You report the call; staff are warned of the pretext.

IGNORE.text: You don’t send anything. Safe.

ESCALATE.text: A manager verifies whether any audit is actually engaged.

scenarios.learn: ‘Officials’ who pressure you for staff data are a classic pretext — verify through a contact you already trust.

scenarios.title: Read Me Your Code
scenarios.text: Right after a login alert, you get a call: “Hi, your bank’s fraud team here. To cancel the suspicious login, read me the 6-digit code we just texted you.”

TRUST.text: You read the code aloud. That code was the key — the attacker completes their login.

VERIFY.text: You hang up and call the bank on the number on your card. It’s a scam.

REPORT.text: You report the call and freeze the account.

IGNORE.text: You hang up. Safe.

ESCALATE.text: You loop in your bank’s verified fraud line.

scenarios.learn: No real bank or service ever asks you to read back a one-time code — that code unlocks your account.

scenarios.title: The Monthly Invoice
scenarios.text: A regular monthly invoice lands in the accounts inbox from a supplier you’ve used for years. Same bank details as always, the usual amount, and it matches an existing purchase order.

TRUST.text: You pay it through the normal process. The supplier is paid on time and the relationship stays healthy.

VERIFY.text: You quickly match it against the purchase order before paying. Prudent — costs a moment.

REPORT.text: You flag it as fraud. Security investigates, finds nothing, and a real supplier is left waiting.

IGNORE.text: You don’t pay. The genuine supplier chases late fees and the relationship sours.

ESCALATE.text: You bump a routine payment up the chain. Fine, just slower than it needed to be.

scenarios.learn: Not everything is an attack. A known supplier, normal channel, unchanged details and a matching PO are signs it’s genuine — and paying on time matters too.

scenarios.title: Patch Tonight
scenarios.text: A message in the official IT channel says a security update will install automatically tonight. No action needed, no links, and it never asks for your password.

TRUST.text: You let it run. Your machine stays patched and protected.

VERIFY.text: You confirm it really came from the IT channel. Quick and sensible.

REPORT.text: You report it as phishing. Security spends time confirming their own genuine notice.

IGNORE.text: No action was needed anyway — no harm done.

ESCALATE.text: You escalate a routine notice. Unnecessary, but harmless.

scenarios.best: TRUST (no links, no credentials, official channel)
scenarios.learn: Real IT messages come through official channels and never ask for passwords or urgent clicks. Spotting a genuine notice avoids alarm fatigue.

scenarios.title: The First Morning
scenarios.text: On the new hire’s first morning, HR sits with them and sets up payroll directly inside the official HR system — in person, no emails, no external links.

TRUST.text: Payroll is set up correctly and securely. A smooth start.

VERIFY.text: The new hire double-checks they’re in the real HR system. Good habit.

REPORT.text: They report their own legitimate onboarding. Awkward, and it stalls their setup.

IGNORE.text: They put it off. Their own payroll setup is delayed.

ESCALATE.text: Unnecessary for a normal in-person setup.

scenarios.best: TRUST (in person, official system, no links)
scenarios.learn: Compare this with the fake ‘confirm your bank details’ email: official system, in person, no links. Context separates real from fake.

scenarios.title: Their Own Account
scenarios.text: A customer, logged in and identity-verified through your official support portal, asks for a copy of their own account information.

TRUST.text: You provide it through the portal. Good service, happy customer.

VERIFY.text: Their identity is already confirmed by the portal, but a quick check doesn’t hurt.

REPORT.text: You treat a verified customer as a threat. Security finds nothing and the customer is frustrated.

IGNORE.text: You leave a legitimate request hanging. The customer’s experience suffers.

ESCALATE.text: You pass a routine, verified request up the line. Slower service than needed.

scenarios.best: TRUST (identity verified through the official portal)
scenarios.learn: Security shouldn’t block legitimate service. Verified identity through an official channel means you can help with confidence.

scenarios.title: A Tool to Try
scenarios.text: A teammate suggests a tool to tidy up a client list. You check, and it’s already on IT’s approved-software list.

TRUST.text: You use the approved tool. Work gets done and the data stays within sanctioned systems.

VERIFY.text: You confirm it’s on the approved list first — exactly the right check.

REPORT.text: You report an already-approved tool. A quick dead end for security.

IGNORE.text: You avoid a perfectly safe, approved tool and do it the slow way.

ESCALATE.text: Unnecessary — it’s already sanctioned.

scenarios.best: VERIFY it’s approved, then use it
scenarios.learn: The rule isn’t ‘never use online tools’ — it’s ‘use approved ones.’ Knowing what’s sanctioned is the skill.

scenarios.title: The Weekly Report
scenarios.text: Your manager, in your usual work chat thread, asks you to send over a non-sensitive document the two of you share every week.

TRUST.text: You send it. Normal request, normal channel — work moves along.

VERIFY.text: You insist on verifying a routine, non-sensitive request. It’s safe, but it slows things and signals distrust.

REPORT.text: You report your own manager’s normal request. Confusing for everyone.

IGNORE.text: You ignore a reasonable ask from your manager. Not a great look.

ESCALATE.text: You escalate a routine request — unnecessary friction.

scenarios.best: TRUST (normal channel, normal request, nothing sensitive)
scenarios.learn: Constant suspicion of normal requests grinds work to a halt. Save verification for the red flags: urgency, secrecy, sensitive data, or an unusual channel.

scenarios.title: The Urgent Invoice
scenarios.text: An invoice from a known supplier arrives — bank details unchanged, but the amount is noticeably higher than usual and the tone is pushier and more urgent than their normal emails.

TRUST.text: You pay it as-is. The figure was padded from a compromised supplier mailbox — partly avoidable.

VERIFY.text: One call to the supplier on a known number clears it up before any money moves.

REPORT.text: You escalate to security. A bit heavy for one odd detail, but no harm.

IGNORE.text: You sit on it. If it was genuine, the supplier is now chasing you.

ESCALATE.text: You route it for a second approval — reasonable for an unusual amount.

scenarios.best: VERIFY — one quick call resolves the doubt
scenarios.learn: When something is mostly normal but one detail is off (amount, tone, urgency), a 30-second check beats both blindly paying and crying fraud.

scenarios.title: Recruiter in Your Inbox
scenarios.text: A recruiter messages you with a plausible-looking role and asks you to send your CV and contact details. It might be a real opportunity — or a way to harvest your information.

TRUST.text: You send a full CV with personal details to an unverified stranger. Possibly fine, possibly harvested.

VERIFY.text: You check the recruiter’s profile and the company before sharing anything. Sensible.

REPORT.text: You report it. If it was genuine, you may have flagged a real recruiter — minor noise.

IGNORE.text: You ignore it. Safe, though you might pass up a real opportunity.

ESCALATE.text: Not really an escalation matter.

scenarios.best: VERIFY (check the profile and company first)
scenarios.learn: Not malicious by default, not safe by default. Verify the source before handing over personal information.

scenarios.title: Sign In Again
scenarios.text: Right after a genuine software update, an app asks you to sign in again. It could be a normal re-authentication — or a fake prompt placed to catch your password.

TRUST.text: You type your password straight into the popup. If it was fake, it’s now stolen.

VERIFY.text: You close the popup and sign in by opening the app yourself. Safe either way.

REPORT.text: You report it. If real, a little noise; no harm done.

IGNORE.text: You dismiss it and can’t use the app until you sign in.

ESCALATE.text: You ask IT — fine, if a touch slow.

scenarios.best: VERIFY (sign in through the app yourself, not the popup)
scenarios.learn: Re-auth prompts are often real — but only ever enter credentials somewhere you navigated to yourself.

scenarios.title: Did You Send This?
scenarios.text: A coworker drops a document link in chat. You weren’t expecting it, but it’s plausibly work-related and they often share files.

TRUST.text: You click it. Their account had been hijacked and the link was malicious — partly avoidable.

VERIFY.text: A quick ‘did you send this?’ confirms it before you click. Two seconds well spent.

REPORT.text: You report it without asking. If genuine, a small false alarm.

IGNORE.text: You skip it. Safe, but you might miss real work.

ESCALATE.text: You loop in IT — a bit much before a simple check.

scenarios.learn: Even real colleagues get hijacked. A two-second check before clicking an unexpected link costs nothing.

scenarios.title: Forward to Everyone
scenarios.text: A well-meaning colleague forwards you a warning: “URGENT — forward this to everyone or Microsoft will shut our email down tomorrow!” It’s a classic chain-mail hoax.

TRUST.text: You forward it company-wide. Now everyone’s inbox carries the panic.

VERIFY.text: You spend time ‘checking’ an obvious hoax. Harmless, but wasted effort.

REPORT.text: You raise a security ticket for a harmless chain email. Minor noise.

IGNORE.text: You delete it and don’t pass it on. The hoax stops with you.

ESCALATE.text: You escalate a hoax to management. Overkill.

scenarios.learn: Hoaxes survive by being forwarded. Don’t pass them on, don’t panic — just delete.

scenarios.title: Book a Demo
scenarios.text: An unsolicited but genuine-looking email from a software vendor wants to book a demo and sell you their product. It’s not a scam — just spam you didn’t ask for.

TRUST.text: You engage, and invite a steady stream of follow-up spam.

VERIFY.text: You research a cold sales email. Time you won’t get back.

REPORT.text: You report marketing spam as phishing. Security checks it, finds nothing, and your real reports look less urgent.

IGNORE.text: You delete it (or unsubscribe). Done — it was never a security matter.

ESCALATE.text: You escalate junk mail. Unnecessary.

scenarios.learn: Not every unwanted email is an attack. Marketing spam gets deleted or unsubscribed — it’s not a security incident.

scenarios.title: The Rude Comment
scenarios.text: On the company’s public social account, an anonymous user posts a rude, baiting comment clearly designed to provoke a reaction.

TRUST.text: You fire back in public. The argument escalates and reflects badly on the brand.

VERIFY.text: There’s nothing here to verify.

REPORT.text: You report a single rude comment as a security threat — it isn’t one.

IGNORE.text: You don’t take the bait. With no audience, the comment fizzles out.

ESCALATE.text: You escalate a one-off troll to comms. Save that for real harassment or impersonation.

scenarios.learn: Public trolls want a reaction. Don’t engage — escalate only if it turns into harassment or impersonation.

scenarios.title: A Stranger Wants to Connect
scenarios.text: You get a connection request from someone you don’t know — no mutual contacts, a thin generic profile. It isn’t impersonating anyone; it’s just a stranger.

TRUST.text: You accept and chat freely, handing a stranger a window into your work life.

VERIFY.text: You investigate a random request at length. Disproportionate.

REPORT.text: You report an ordinary stranger as a threat. False alarm.

IGNORE.text: You leave it or decline. No harm, no fuss.

ESCALATE.text: You escalate a connection request. Overkill.

scenarios.learn: A stranger’s request isn’t a threat to report — decline or ignore it. Save caution for accounts impersonating real people.

scenarios.title: You’ve Won!
scenarios.text: An email already sitting in your junk folder shouts: “CONGRATULATIONS!!! You’ve WON $1,000,000 in a lottery you never entered. Claim within 24 hours!” It’s riddled with typos.

TRUST.text: You click through and start handing over details on a scam page.

VERIFY.text: You waste time ‘checking’ a lottery you never entered.

REPORT.text: You file a ticket for junk the filter already caught. Save reporting for convincing, targeted messages.

IGNORE.text: You delete it. Obvious junk, already filtered — no ticket needed.

ESCALATE.text: You escalate obvious spam. No need.

scenarios.learn: Obvious junk that’s already in spam doesn’t need a ticket — delete it. Reserve reporting for messages that look targeted or convincing.

scenarios.title: The Journalist Calls
scenarios.text: A journalist phones and asks you to confirm whether the company suffered a data breach last week. You’re not certain, and it isn’t your place to speak for the company.

TRUST.text: You comment on the company’s behalf without authority — your words become the story.

VERIFY.text: You can’t verify a breach yourself, and that isn’t the point.

REPORT.text: You file an IT ticket — wrong channel. This is a comms and leadership matter.

IGNORE.text: You brush them off and tell nobody. Leadership is blindsided when it runs.

ESCALATE.text: You take a message and pass it straight to management / comms. Exactly right.

scenarios.learn: Press, legal and regulator questions go straight to management — never improvise on the company’s behalf, and never just sit on them.

scenarios.title: A Request for Records
scenarios.text: A request arrives — confirmed genuine through proper channels — from a regulator asking for a set of customer records. It’s real, but releasing customer data is a big, high-stakes step.

TRUST.text: Even though it’s genuine, you release customer data on your own authority — a serious overstep.

VERIFY.text: Confirming it’s real is wise — but once it is, you still must escalate, not act alone.

REPORT.text: This isn’t a phishing report; it needs an authority decision.

IGNORE.text: You sit on a legitimate regulator request. That invites real trouble.

ESCALATE.text: You route it to management / legal / your data-protection lead for sign-off. Correct.

scenarios.learn: Even a legitimate request for sensitive data needs proper authorisation. Confirm it’s real, then escalate — don’t release data solo.

scenarios.title: Working From Home
scenarios.text: You notice a teammate routinely emailing unencrypted customer spreadsheets to their personal Gmail so they can work from home. They’re not malicious — just careless.

TRUST.text: You decide it’s none of your business — and quietly condone the risk.

VERIFY.text: There’s nothing to verify; the behaviour is plain to see.

REPORT.text: You open a formal security incident on a careless but well-meaning colleague — heavier than it needs to be.

IGNORE.text: You let it slide. The data keeps leaving the company unprotected.

ESCALATE.text: You raise it calmly with your manager / security, so it’s handled and the person gets support.

scenarios.best: ESCALATE (raise it through the right channel)
scenarios.learn: Insider mistakes are a people problem, not a phishing report. Raise them through your manager or security so they’re handled constructively.

scenarios.title: The Access Request
scenarios.text: A new manager asks — politely, through the proper ticket system — for full admin access to a sensitive system ‘to do their job.’ The request looks legitimate and follows process, but it’s a big privilege to grant.

TRUST.text: You hand over broad admin access with no review — a real exposure, however well-meant.

VERIFY.text: Confirming identity is fine, but the real question is how much access is appropriate.

REPORT.text: Nothing here to report — it isn’t a threat, it’s an authorisation call.

IGNORE.text: You quietly block a legitimate-looking request and stall the new manager’s work.

ESCALATE.text: You route it to the system owner for a least-privilege review and approval. Exactly how big grants should work.

scenarios.learn: Genuine-looking, high-impact requests still need the right approval. Escalate big access grants for review rather than granting — or blocking — them yourself.

scenarios.title: A Quiet Demand
scenarios.text: An email addressed to you personally threatens to leak company data unless you arrange a payment quietly, today. It feels aimed and credible.

TRUST.text: You engage and arrange payment alone — funding a criminal with no guarantee of anything.

VERIFY.text: There’s no safe way to ‘check’ an extortion demand yourself.

REPORT.text: Logging it helps, but a threat like this needs leadership and incident response, not just a ticket.

IGNORE.text: You bury a credible threat against the company. If it’s real, nobody is ready.

ESCALATE.text: You take it straight to leadership and incident response. The right people respond as a team.

scenarios.learn: A credible threat against the company goes to leadership and incident response fast — don’t pay, don’t negotiate, and don’t sit on it alone.

roles.name: Receptionist / Front Desk
roles.description: The first point of contact for every visitor, call and delivery.
roles.ability: Gatekeeper — once per game, auto-resolve any physical/visitor scenario as VERIFY (prevented).

roles.name: HR Manager
roles.description: Holds the personal data of everyone in the company.
roles.targetedBy: Fake onboarding, data requests, payroll phishing.
roles.ability: Records Lock — once per game, cancel one Reputation loss from a data-leak outcome.

roles.name: Finance Officer
roles.description: Controls payments — the prize attackers want most.
roles.targetedBy: Invoice fraud, business-email compromise, deepfake transfer calls.
roles.ability: Dual Control — once per game, force VERIFY on a money scenario (no Funds lost that round).

roles.name: CEO / Executive
roles.description: High authority and a public profile — often the person attackers impersonate.
roles.targetedBy: Impersonation and deepfakes (as the faked person), targeted phishing.
roles.ability: Set the Tone — once per game, reduce the team’s Stress by 10.

roles.name: Intern / New Hire
roles.description: Eager to help and still learning what’s normal.
roles.targetedBy: Pressure tactics, onboarding scams, oversharing online.
roles.ability: Beginner’s Question — once per game, ask the facilitator one yes/no question before deciding.

roles.name: IT / Developer
roles.description: Keeps the systems running and is trusted with access.
roles.targetedBy: Vishing (‘IT support’), malicious files, shadow IT.
roles.ability: Patch It — once per game, turn one REPORT into a guaranteed ‘attack prevented.’

roles.name: Salesperson
roles.description: Always on the move, with lots of external contact and devices.
roles.targetedBy: Public Wi-Fi, lost devices, QR scams.
roles.ability: Road Warrior — once per game, ignore one Stress penalty.

events.title: IT Is Overloaded
events.text: Any REPORT this round takes effect next round instead of now.

events.title: The CEO Is on Vacation
events.text: You can’t verify with the CEO this round: +5 Stress on any CEO-related scenario.

events.title: Journalist Calls
events.text: If Reputation is below 60, lose 5 more Reputation. Otherwise, no effect.

events.title: Payday Rush
events.text: Finance is slammed — the next money scenario adds +5 Stress.

events.title: Audit Week
events.text: Good security calls this round earn +5 extra Security Score.

events.title: Holiday Scam Surge
events.text: Draw and resolve TWO scenario cards this round.

events.title: Phishing Simulation
events.text: Your own security team sends a test. Resolve the next scenario with no Funds penalty.

events.title: New-Starter Week
events.text: Every scenario this round targets the Intern / New Hire if possible.

events.title: Backups Verified
events.text: Reward: the next REPORT on a ransomware/malware scenario fully prevents it.

events.title: VIP Visit
events.text: Reputation is fragile — any TRUST mistake this round costs +5 extra Reputation.
